prompt: In John 8, where did Jesus go before returning early to teach in the temple?
choices_json: The Mount of Olives
the treasury
the middle of the court
the temple steps
correct_answer: The Mount of Olives
explanation: John says Jesus went to the Mount of Olives before coming back to the temple early in the morning (John 8:1-2).

prompt: Who brought the woman caught in adultery to Jesus in John 8?
choices_json: the temple guards
The scribes and the Pharisees
the Jews who had believed him
the disciples
correct_answer: The scribes and the Pharisees
explanation: The scribes and Pharisees brought the woman and set her in the middle (John 8:3).

prompt: According to her accusers, what had the woman in John 8 been caught doing?
choices_json: seeking to kill Jesus
walking in darkness
committing adultery
bearing false testimony in the treasury
correct_answer: committing adultery
explanation: They said they found her in adultery, in the very act (John 8:4).

prompt: What did Jesus do after they kept questioning him about the woman in John 8?
choices_json: He left the temple court immediately.
He called for witnesses from the crowd.
He asked them to bring Abraham as proof.
He stooped down and wrote on the ground.
correct_answer: He stooped down and wrote on the ground.
explanation: When they tested him, Jesus stooped down and wrote on the ground with his finger (John 8:6, 8).

prompt: Which question did Jesus ask the woman after her accusers were gone?
choices_json: Where are your accusers?
Who touched me?
Do you believe in the Son of God?
Will you also go away?
correct_answer: Where are your accusers?
explanation: After the accusers left, Jesus asked, 'Where are your accusers? Did no one condemn you?' (John 8:10).

prompt: What title does Jesus use for himself when he speaks in John 8:12?
choices_json: the shepherd of the sheep
the light of the world
the bread from heaven
the true vine
correct_answer: the light of the world
explanation: Jesus says, 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12).

prompt: Where in the temple was Jesus speaking when John says no one arrested him?
choices_json: the Mount of Olives
Solomon's porch
the treasury
the pool of Siloam
correct_answer: the treasury
explanation: John says Jesus spoke these words in the treasury as he taught in the temple (John 8:20).

prompt: What challenge did Jesus give the accusers before they left the woman in John 8?
choices_json: If a person keeps my word, he will never see death.
If you remain in my word, the truth will make you free indeed forever.
Neither do I condemn you. Go your way.
He who is without sin among you, let him throw the first stone at her.
correct_answer: He who is without sin among you, let him throw the first stone at her.
explanation: Jesus answered their trap with the challenge about the sinless person casting the first stone (John 8:7).

prompt: In what order did the accusers leave after hearing Jesus in John 8?
choices_json: one by one, beginning from the oldest
all at once after the guards arrived
youngest first, then the oldest
in groups after questioning the woman again
correct_answer: one by one, beginning from the oldest
explanation: John says they went out one by one, beginning from the oldest (John 8:9).

prompt: What instruction did Jesus give the woman after saying he did not condemn her?
choices_json: Return tomorrow with two witnesses.
Go your way. From now on, sin no more.
Offer the required sacrifice in the treasury.
Remain here until your accusers come back.
correct_answer: Go your way. From now on, sin no more.
explanation: Jesus told her to go and sin no more (John 8:11).

prompt: According to the law Jesus cites in John 8, the testimony of how many people is valid?
choices_json: one righteous judge
three temple officers
two people
seven elders
correct_answer: two people
explanation: Jesus says their law counts the testimony of two people as valid (John 8:17).

prompt: What did Jesus say would happen unless they believed that 'I am he'?
choices_json: They would lose their place in the synagogue.
They would never enter the treasury again.
They would be sent back to Galilee.
They would die in their sins.
correct_answer: They would die in their sins.
explanation: Jesus warns that without believing, they would die in their sins (John 8:24).

prompt: What did Jesus say would mark those Jews as truly his disciples in John 8?
choices_json: remaining in his word
guarding the treasury
tracing their line to Abraham
refusing to speak in public
correct_answer: remaining in his word
explanation: Jesus says, 'If you remain in my word, then you are truly my disciples' (John 8:31).

prompt: What did Jesus say the truth would do in John 8?
choices_json: raise the temple in three days
make you free
drive out every Roman ruler
keep you from ever suffering
correct_answer: make you free
explanation: Jesus says, 'You will know the truth, and the truth will make you free' (John 8:32).

prompt: Why does John say the scribes and Pharisees brought the adultery case to Jesus?
choices_json: They wanted him to replace Moses's command.
They were asking him to appoint new temple guards.
They were testing him so they could accuse him.
They hoped he would leave the treasury at once.
correct_answer: They were testing him so they could accuse him.
explanation: John explains that they said this testing him, so they might have something to accuse him of (John 8:6).

prompt: What does the accusers' departure in John 8 suggest about Jesus's challenge?
choices_json: It proved the woman had not sinned.
It showed the law no longer mattered.
It convinced them Jesus was not from Galilee.
It exposed their own guilt.
correct_answer: It exposed their own guilt.
explanation: They were convicted by their conscience and left one by one (John 8:9).

prompt: Why does Jesus say his testimony is true even if he testifies about himself?
choices_json: He knows where he came from and where he is going.
The people in the temple had already agreed that his testimony was valid.
He learned the ruling from the scribes.
The Romans had already certified his claims.
correct_answer: He knows where he came from and where he is going.
explanation: Jesus says his testimony is true because he knows where he came from and where he is going (John 8:14).

prompt: What misunderstanding appears when Jesus says, 'Where I go, you can't come'?
choices_json: They think he plans to return to Nazareth.
They think he may kill himself.
They think he is going to the pool of Siloam.
They think he wants to join the temple guard.
correct_answer: They think he may kill himself.
explanation: The Jews respond by asking whether he will kill himself (John 8:21-22).

prompt: In John 8, what kind of freedom is Jesus mainly talking about when he answers Abraham's offspring?
choices_json: freedom from Roman taxation
freedom to leave Judea for Galilee
freedom from slavery to sin
freedom from appearing in the treasury
correct_answer: freedom from slavery to sin
explanation: Jesus explains that everyone who commits sin is the bondservant of sin (John 8:33-36).

prompt: Why does Jesus say his hearers are not acting like Abraham's true children?
choices_json: They forgot the route to the Mount of Olives.
They never show any public proof that Abraham belongs to their family line.
They have never heard of the patriarchs.
They are seeking to kill the one who told them truth from God.
correct_answer: They are seeking to kill the one who told them truth from God.
explanation: Jesus says Abraham did not do what they are doing, since they seek to kill him after hearing truth from God (John 8:39-40).

prompt: What does Jesus say keeps some of his hearers from understanding his speech in John 8?
choices_json: They cannot hear his word.
They arrived too late to hear the first half.
They are speaking the wrong language in the temple.
They are distracted by the woman in the crowd.
correct_answer: They cannot hear his word.
explanation: Jesus says they do not understand because they cannot hear his word (John 8:43).

prompt: In the flow of John 8, what does Jesus's claim to be 'the light of the world' stand against?
choices_json: the shortage of wine at a wedding feast
walking in darkness and lacking the light of life
the storm on the Sea of Galilee
the rebuilding of the temple treasury
correct_answer: walking in darkness and lacking the light of life
explanation: Jesus says the one who follows him will not walk in darkness but will have the light of life (John 8:12).

prompt: Why does Jesus pair his own witness with the Father's witness in the dispute over testimony?
choices_json: to argue that every human witness in Israel has become permanently irrelevant
to replace Scripture with a new temple rule
to show his identity is confirmed within the Father-Son relationship
to deny that the law ever required witnesses
correct_answer: to show his identity is confirmed within the Father-Son relationship
explanation: Jesus says both he and the Father who sent him testify about him (John 8:17-18).

prompt: How does John 8 use the language of slavery and sonship?
choices_json: Roman rule enslaves, but the temple tax can release people.
Only physical descent from Abraham makes someone a son forever.
The chapter is mainly explaining household law in Jerusalem.
Sin enslaves, but the Son can grant lasting freedom.
correct_answer: Sin enslaves, but the Son can grant lasting freedom.
explanation: Jesus contrasts the bondservant with the son and says the Son makes people free indeed (John 8:34-36).

prompt: What does Jesus's argument about Abraham mainly expose in John 8?
choices_json: Physical descent is not the same as doing Abraham's works.
Abraham never received any promise from God.
Lineage can be purchased with the right offering.
The treasury replaced the patriarchs as the center of Israel's entire story.
correct_answer: Physical descent is not the same as doing Abraham's works.
explanation: Jesus says if they were Abraham's children, they would do Abraham's works (John 8:39).

prompt: Why does Jesus call the devil 'a murderer from the beginning' and 'the father of lies' in this chapter?
choices_json: He is explaining Roman military policy in Judea.
He is exposing a murderous unbelief that reflects a false spiritual father.
He is identifying Abraham as the source of every lie.
He is saying that every false statement is worse than murder by itself.
correct_answer: He is exposing a murderous unbelief that reflects a false spiritual father.
explanation: Jesus connects their desire to kill him and reject truth with the devil's character (John 8:44-45).

prompt: What does 'before Abraham came into existence, I AM' communicate inside John 8?
choices_json: Jesus is only saying he is almost fifty years old.
Jesus is retracting his words about the Father.
Jesus claims existence and identity beyond Abraham's timeframe.
Jesus is claiming Abraham wrote the whole temple law by himself long ago.
correct_answer: Jesus claims existence and identity beyond Abraham's timeframe.
explanation: The statement places Jesus before Abraham rather than after him (John 8:57-58).

prompt: Why do the hearers pick up stones at the end of John 8?
choices_json: They want to rebuild the treasury wall.
They are preparing to carry out the woman's sentence after the debate.
They need stones for a memorial to Abraham.
They hear Jesus's claim as a grave offense against God.
correct_answer: They hear Jesus's claim as a grave offense against God.
explanation: After Jesus says, 'I AM,' they take up stones to throw at him (John 8:58-59).

prompt: How do Jesus's words to the woman in John 8 hold mercy and holiness together?
choices_json: He refuses to condemn her while still telling her to leave her sin.
He excuses adultery as a small matter.
He hands judgment back to the crowd.
He ignores her and rebukes only the scribes.
correct_answer: He refuses to condemn her while still telling her to leave her sin.
explanation: Jesus says, 'Neither do I condemn you,' and then, 'From now on, sin no more' (John 8:11).

prompt: What does John 8 suggest really defines a person's spiritual father?
choices_json: the town listed on a family register
whose desires and works that person follows
the size of the household inheritance
the place where someone first learned the law
correct_answer: whose desires and works that person follows
explanation: Jesus reasons from actions and desires when he contrasts Abraham, God, and the devil (John 8:39-44).

prompt: Why is 'If the Son makes you free, you will be free indeed' more than a political promise in John 8?
choices_json: The crowd is only asking about Roman taxes.
Jesus is planning an armed revolt in the temple.
The chapter has already defined bondage as slavery to sin.
The dispute is really about gaining travel rights and public status in Galilee.
correct_answer: The chapter has already defined bondage as slavery to sin.
explanation: Jesus defines the deeper slavery as bondage to sin before speaking of true freedom (John 8:34-36).

prompt: How does truth function in Jesus's discourse with those who had believed him?
choices_json: Truth is a secret reserved for the temple officers.
Truth arrives only after Abraham returns to Jerusalem.
Truth means avoiding every judgment and accusation.
Abiding in his word leads to truth that liberates.
correct_answer: Abiding in his word leads to truth that liberates.
explanation: Jesus connects remaining in his word, knowing the truth, and being made free (John 8:31-32).

prompt: What role does Abraham play in the chapter's final dispute?
choices_json: He becomes a witness against hostile claims of covenant privilege.
He appears physically in the temple crowd to defend Jesus before the stones are raised.
He replaces Moses as a new lawgiver in Jerusalem.
He is blamed for bringing the woman to Jesus.
correct_answer: He becomes a witness against hostile claims of covenant privilege.
explanation: Jesus uses Abraham to expose the gap between claimed ancestry and actual response to God (John 8:39-40, 56).

prompt: How does the chapter connect Jesus's words and the Father's presence?
choices_json: Jesus says the Father withdrew from him in the temple.
Jesus speaks what the Father taught him and does what pleases him.
Jesus claims the Father's witness depends on how positively the crowd responds.
Jesus separates his mission from the Father's will.
correct_answer: Jesus speaks what the Father taught him and does what pleases him.
explanation: Jesus says he speaks as the Father taught him and that the One who sent him is with him (John 8:28-29).

prompt: What does Jesus passing through the crowd after they take up stones underscore in John 8?
choices_json: The crowd immediately becomes his disciples.
The accusers of the woman return to defend him.
His departure still unfolds on his terms, not theirs.
The treasury officials reverse the whole dispute.
correct_answer: His departure still unfolds on his terms, not theirs.
explanation: John ends the chapter with Jesus hidden and going out of the temple after they take up stones (John 8:59).

prompt: In John 9, whom did Jesus see as he passed by?
choices_json: a lame man by a pool
a Samaritan woman by a well
a ruler's son near death
a man blind from birth
correct_answer: a man blind from birth
explanation: John 9 opens with Jesus seeing a man blind from birth (John 9:1).

prompt: What question did the disciples ask Jesus about the blind man in John 9?
choices_json: Who sinned, this man or his parents?
Why was he begging at the temple gate?
Should he be taken first to the Pharisees?
Will he wash in the Jordan or in Siloam?
correct_answer: Who sinned, this man or his parents?
explanation: The disciples ask whether the man or his parents sinned so that he was born blind (John 9:2).

prompt: What did Jesus make before putting it on the blind man's eyes?
choices_json: oil from the temple lamps
mud with his saliva
water from the pool of Siloam
dust mixed with wine
correct_answer: mud with his saliva
explanation: Jesus spat on the ground, made mud with the saliva, and put it on the man's eyes (John 9:6).

prompt: Where did Jesus send the blind man to wash?
choices_json: the treasury fountain
the Jordan beyond the city
the pool of Siloam
Bethany near the Mount of Olives
correct_answer: the pool of Siloam
explanation: Jesus told him to wash in the pool of Siloam (John 9:7).

prompt: On what day did Jesus make the mud and open the man's eyes?
choices_json: the Feast of Dedication
Passover morning
the day after a wedding feast
the Sabbath
correct_answer: the Sabbath
explanation: John notes that it was a Sabbath when Jesus made the mud and opened his eyes (John 9:14).

prompt: To whom was the healed man first brought for questioning in John 9?
choices_json: the Pharisees
the Sadducees
the Roman governor
the chief tax collectors
correct_answer: the Pharisees
explanation: After the healing, people brought the man who had been blind to the Pharisees (John 9:13).

prompt: How did the healed man first describe Jesus to the neighbors in John 9?
choices_json: the ruler of the synagogue
a man called Jesus
the Son of David king
a priest from Jerusalem
correct_answer: a man called Jesus
explanation: He tells the neighbors, 'A man called Jesus made mud' (John 9:11).

prompt: Why did Jesus say the man in John 9 had been born blind?
choices_json: that his parents might repent in public
that he would become a public warning to every beggar in Jerusalem
that the works of God might be revealed in him
that the Pharisees could test the Sabbath
correct_answer: that the works of God might be revealed in him
explanation: Jesus says the blindness was an occasion for the works of God to be revealed in him (John 9:3).

prompt: What does John say the name Siloam means?
choices_json: Peace
Harvest
Mercy
Sent
correct_answer: Sent
explanation: John adds that Siloam means 'Sent' (John 9:7).

prompt: What were the neighbors debating about the healed man in John 9?
choices_json: whether he was the same man who used to sit and beg
whether he should become a Pharisee
whether his parents lived near the temple
whether he had learned his story from John the Baptist's followers
correct_answer: whether he was the same man who used to sit and beg
explanation: The neighbors argue over whether he is the same man who had sat and begged (John 9:8-9).

prompt: What short testimony did the healed man give when the Pharisees asked how he received sight?
choices_json: He touched my cloak, and I rose up.
He put mud on my eyes, I washed, and I see.
He spoke a word in Cana, and I was healed there.
He told me to fast, and then my sight returned.
correct_answer: He put mud on my eyes, I washed, and I see.
explanation: The man summarizes the healing this way when speaking to the Pharisees (John 9:15).

prompt: What did the healed man's parents confirm to the Pharisees in John 9?
choices_json: Jesus healed him before all the rulers.
He had never truly been blind at all.
This is our son, and he was born blind.
The neighbors made up the whole story.
correct_answer: This is our son, and he was born blind.
explanation: His parents confirm both his identity and that he was born blind (John 9:20).

prompt: Why were the man's parents careful not to say who healed him?
choices_json: They were traveling beyond the Jordan that night.
They had not recognized their own son.
They wanted the Pharisees to reward them first.
They feared being put out of the synagogue.
correct_answer: They feared being put out of the synagogue.
explanation: John explains that they feared the Jews because confessing Jesus as Christ could bring expulsion from the synagogue (John 9:22-23).

prompt: When asked what he thought about Jesus, what title did the healed man use?
choices_json: He is a prophet.
He is a Sabbath breaker only.
He is the gatekeeper of the sheep.
He is a Roman messenger.
correct_answer: He is a prophet.
explanation: When pressed, the man says of Jesus, 'He is a prophet' (John 9:17).

prompt: What does the blind man's trip to Siloam show about his response to Jesus?
choices_json: He delayed until the Pharisees approved.
He obeyed Jesus's command and received sight.
He refused until his parents forced him.
He waited until nightfall before responding to Jesus's command at all.
correct_answer: He obeyed Jesus's command and received sight.
explanation: Jesus told him to go and wash, and he went, washed, and came back seeing (John 9:7).

prompt: Why were some Pharisees divided over Jesus in John 9?
choices_json: They were arguing over who owned the pool of Siloam.
They disagreed about whether the parents lived in Jerusalem.
The Sabbath objection clashed with a sign they could not explain away.
They had not heard that the man used to beg.
correct_answer: The Sabbath objection clashed with a sign they could not explain away.
explanation: Some object to Jesus not keeping the Sabbath, while others ask how a sinner could do such signs (John 9:16).

prompt: What does the parents' answer reveal about the pressure surrounding Jesus in John 9?
choices_json: Everyone in Jerusalem was already following Jesus.
The synagogue leaders had approved the miracle.
Roman officials were rewarding anyone who testified.
Fear of expulsion made open confession costly.
correct_answer: Fear of expulsion made open confession costly.
explanation: Their guarded answer is explained by fear of being put out of the synagogue (John 9:22-23).

prompt: How does the healed man's witness develop across John 9?
choices_json: He moves from calling Jesus a man to calling him a prophet and then worshiping him.
He gradually backs away from his first story until he denies that anything happened.
He blames his parents and avoids Jesus entirely.
He starts by worshiping Jesus and ends by denying him.
correct_answer: He moves from calling Jesus a man to calling him a prophet and then worshiping him.
explanation: The chapter traces the man's growing recognition of Jesus from verse 11 to verse 38 (John 9:11, 17, 33, 38).

prompt: Why is the statement 'Though I was blind, now I see' so forceful in the hearing?
choices_json: It proves he has mastered every prophecy in Scripture from beginning to end.
It anchors the debate in undeniable personal experience.
It settles every Sabbath dispute by itself.
It tells the Pharisees exactly where Jesus is from.
correct_answer: It anchors the debate in undeniable personal experience.
explanation: The man's testimony keeps the debate fixed on what actually happened to him (John 9:25).

prompt: What irony appears when the authorities say they do not know where Jesus comes from?
choices_json: They have forgotten which street leads to Siloam.
They mistake Jesus for John the Baptist.
They claim ignorance while facing a work that points to God.
They are really asking for travel directions out of the city.
correct_answer: They claim ignorance while facing a work that points to God.
explanation: The healed man marvels that they do not know where Jesus comes from even though he opened his eyes (John 9:30-33).

prompt: Why does Jesus seek out the man after he has been thrown out?
choices_json: to send him back for a third hearing
to appoint him as a temple guard
to return him to begging outside the synagogue as before, without further discussion
to lead him from physical healing into explicit faith in the Son of God
correct_answer: to lead him from physical healing into explicit faith in the Son of God
explanation: After hearing he had been cast out, Jesus finds him and reveals himself so the man may believe (John 9:35-38).

prompt: How does the healing in John 9 embody Jesus's claim, 'I am the light of the world'?
choices_json: The sign gives physical sight while pointing to spiritual illumination.
The sign replaces every temple lamp in Jerusalem.
The sign mainly explains how to use Siloam water.
The sign teaches that blindness is always caused by sin.
correct_answer: The sign gives physical sight while pointing to spiritual illumination.
explanation: Jesus speaks of being the light of the world and then gives sight to the man born blind (John 9:5-7).

prompt: What kind of judgment does Jesus describe at the end of John 9?
choices_json: Every sinner is struck with physical blindness whenever the Sabbath is broken.
Those who admit blindness may see, while those who claim sight become blind.
Only the parents are judged for the man's condition.
Judgment is postponed because no one can respond yet.
correct_answer: Those who admit blindness may see, while those who claim sight become blind.
explanation: Jesus says he came so that those who do not see may see and those who see may become blind (John 9:39-41).

prompt: What does John 9 suggest about true knowledge of Jesus?
choices_json: It depends mainly on family status within the synagogue.
It comes only through approval from the Pharisees.
It grows through obedient response and honest testimony.
It can be gained without any encounter with Jesus.
correct_answer: It grows through obedient response and honest testimony.
explanation: The man's understanding deepens as he obeys, speaks honestly, and finally meets Jesus again (John 9:7, 17, 25, 35-38).

prompt: Why is the healed man's worship important after Jesus reveals himself?
choices_json: It proves he has become the new synagogue ruler over the people who questioned him.
It shows he has forgotten the healing itself.
It means the Pharisees finally agreed with him.
It shows the sign leads to faith and reverence for Jesus.
correct_answer: It shows the sign leads to faith and reverence for Jesus.
explanation: When Jesus reveals himself, the man says, 'Lord, I believe,' and worships him (John 9:35-38).

prompt: What role does synagogue expulsion play in John 9?
choices_json: It shows that confessing Jesus can carry social and religious cost.
It explains why the man loses the healing later and must return for another sign.
It fulfills a Roman law about public begging.
It proves the parents were from a different tribe.
correct_answer: It shows that confessing Jesus can carry social and religious cost.
explanation: Fear of expulsion shapes the parents' response, and the healed man is later thrown out (John 9:22, 34).

prompt: How does John 9 contrast evidence and unbelief?
choices_json: The miracle stays hidden so no one can know what happened.
A public healing is plain, yet leaders still resist its meaning.
The parents deny the healing ever took place.
Jesus refuses to discuss the sign with anyone.
correct_answer: A public healing is plain, yet leaders still resist its meaning.
explanation: The chapter presents repeated testimony to the healing alongside persistent resistance from the authorities (John 9:13-34).

prompt: Why does the chapter keep returning to testimony from neighbors, parents, and the healed man?
choices_json: John wants to avoid mentioning Jesus directly.
The law required a fixed sequence of three Sabbath hearings for every healing case.
The repeated witnesses intensify the case while exposing stubborn unbelief.
The healed man could not remember what happened.
correct_answer: The repeated witnesses intensify the case while exposing stubborn unbelief.
explanation: The repeated questioning piles up evidence even as resistance hardens (John 9:8-34).

prompt: How does Jesus's answer about the blind man's condition reshape the usual link between suffering and sin?
choices_json: He says every disability comes from parental sin.
He teaches that suffering has no meaning at all.
He blames the Pharisees for the man's birth condition.
He rejects a simple blame formula and frames the situation as a setting for God's works.
correct_answer: He rejects a simple blame formula and frames the situation as a setting for God's works.
explanation: Jesus says neither the man nor his parents sinned in the way the disciples assumed, but that God's works might be revealed (John 9:2-3).

prompt: What literary irony dominates John 9 from start to finish?
choices_json: The man without sight comes to see truly, while the self-assured authorities remain blind.
The neighbors become blind and then recover their sight.
Jesus loses track of the healed man and must depend on the neighbors to find him.
The parents understand everything better than anyone else.
correct_answer: The man without sight comes to see truly, while the self-assured authorities remain blind.
explanation: The chapter contrasts growing sight in the healed man with persistent blindness in those who claim to see (John 9:39-41).

prompt: How does John 9 move the healed man toward discipleship?
choices_json: Healing leads him to wealth, office, and honor in the synagogue.
Healing leads to testimony, rejection, revelation, and worship.
Healing leads him back to begging as before.
Healing leads him to deny Jesus in order to stay safe.
correct_answer: Healing leads to testimony, rejection, revelation, and worship.
explanation: The narrative moves from restored sight to public witness, being cast out, and then worshiping Jesus (John 9:11-38).

prompt: What does Jesus mean when he says, 'If you were blind, you would have no sin; but now you say, We see'?
choices_json: Knowing the law automatically removes all guilt.
Physical sight itself makes every person sinful.
Their guilt remains because they claim spiritual sight while rejecting the light before them.
He is excusing the Pharisees because they are sincere.
correct_answer: Their guilt remains because they claim spiritual sight while rejecting the light before them.
explanation: Jesus's point is about claimed spiritual sight, not eyesight alone, and he says their sin remains (John 9:41).

prompt: How does the pool of Siloam contribute to the message of John 9?
choices_json: The pool itself is presented as holier and more powerful than the temple courts.
The pool cures everyone who enters it on the Sabbath.
The pool replaces Jesus as the source of healing.
The man's obedient washing becomes part of the sign that reveals Jesus's mission.
correct_answer: The man's obedient washing becomes part of the sign that reveals Jesus's mission.
explanation: Jesus sends the man to Siloam, and the healing comes as he obeys that word (John 9:7).

prompt: What kind of discipleship does the healed man's example model in John 9?
choices_json: speaking truth about Jesus even when authorities threaten exclusion
keeping silent until the crowd approves
following Jesus only after receiving official status
trusting signs but never confessing Christ
correct_answer: speaking truth about Jesus even when authorities threaten exclusion
explanation: The healed man keeps speaking truthfully about Jesus even after hostile questioning and expulsion (John 9:25-34).

prompt: How do 'the works of God' frame the whole chapter?
choices_json: They refer only to the mud itself and not to anything that follows afterward.
They begin in the healing and continue in the exposure of spiritual blindness.
They describe the Pharisees' investigation process.
They end before the man speaks to anyone.
correct_answer: They begin in the healing and continue in the exposure of spiritual blindness.
explanation: Jesus introduces the chapter by speaking of God's works, and the chapter closes with those works revealing who truly sees (John 9:3-5, 39-41).

prompt: In John 10, who does Jesus say climbs into the sheepfold some other way?
choices_json: the shepherd of the sheep
the gatekeeper
a thief and a robber
one of the sheep by name
correct_answer: a thief and a robber
explanation: Jesus says the one who climbs in some other way is a thief and a robber (John 10:1).

prompt: Who enters the sheepfold by the door in Jesus's picture?
choices_json: the hired hand
the wolf
the stranger
the shepherd of the sheep
correct_answer: the shepherd of the sheep
explanation: Jesus says the one who enters by the door is the shepherd of the sheep (John 10:2).

prompt: What do the sheep recognize in order to follow the shepherd?
choices_json: his voice
his clothing
his hometown
his staff's height
correct_answer: his voice
explanation: The sheep follow the shepherd because they know his voice (John 10:3-4).

prompt: What image does Jesus use for himself in John 10:9?
choices_json: the lampstand
the door
the cornerstone
the vine
correct_answer: the door
explanation: Jesus says plainly, 'I am the door' (John 10:9).

prompt: What does the good shepherd do for the sheep in John 10?
choices_json: He sells them for silver.
He hides from every wolf.
He lays down his life for them.
He leaves them to the hired hand.
correct_answer: He lays down his life for them.
explanation: Jesus says the good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11).

prompt: At what feast does the later temple scene in John 10 take place?
choices_json: the Feast of Booths
Passover
the Feast of Firstfruits celebrated later in the temple courts
the Feast of the Dedication
correct_answer: the Feast of the Dedication
explanation: John says it was the Feast of the Dedication at Jerusalem (John 10:22).

prompt: Where did Jesus go after escaping those who tried to seize him in John 10?
choices_json: beyond the Jordan where John had first been baptizing
to the pool of Siloam
back to Cana for another sign
into the treasury to teach again
correct_answer: beyond the Jordan where John had first been baptizing
explanation: Jesus went away beyond the Jordan to the place where John had been baptizing at first (John 10:40).

prompt: What do the sheep do when a stranger speaks in John 10?
choices_json: They gather around him.
They flee from him.
They crown him shepherd.
They lead him to the gatekeeper.
correct_answer: They flee from him.
explanation: Jesus says the sheep will by no means follow a stranger, but will flee from him (John 10:5).

prompt: According to Jesus, what will a person find if he enters by the door?
choices_json: hidden treasure
a judge's seat
pasture
new stone tablets
correct_answer: pasture
explanation: Jesus says the one who enters by him will find pasture (John 10:9).

prompt: What three actions does the thief come to do in John 10?
choices_json: teach, heal, and restore
gather, guard, and feed
call, send, and bless
steal, kill, and destroy
correct_answer: steal, kill, and destroy
explanation: Jesus contrasts the thief's destructive purpose with his own life-giving mission (John 10:10).

prompt: Why does the hired hand run away when he sees the wolf coming?
choices_json: He does not own the sheep and does not care for them.
He is guarding the treasury that day.
He has been sent away to prepare for the feast in Jerusalem.
He wants the shepherd to praise his speed.
correct_answer: He does not own the sheep and does not care for them.
explanation: Jesus says the hired hand flees because he is a hired hand and does not care for the sheep (John 10:12-13).

prompt: What will happen to the other sheep Jesus says are not of this fold?
choices_json: They will remain in a separate fold forever.
They will become one flock with one shepherd.
They will be scattered by the next wolf.
They will follow John instead of Jesus.
correct_answer: They will become one flock with one shepherd.
explanation: Jesus says he must bring them also, and they will become one flock with one shepherd (John 10:16).

prompt: What authority does Jesus say he has regarding his life in John 10?
choices_json: to hide it permanently from his enemies
to hand it to the hired hand for safekeeping
to lay it down and to take it again
to postpone it until the feast is over
correct_answer: to lay it down and to take it again
explanation: Jesus says he has authority both to lay down his life and to take it again (John 10:18).

prompt: What charge do the Jews make after Jesus says, 'I and the Father are one'?
choices_json: that he broke the gate of the sheepfold
that he deserted Moses at the feast
that he refused to answer them about John
that he, being a man, makes himself God
correct_answer: that he, being a man, makes himself God
explanation: They accuse Jesus of blasphemy because, as they say, he makes himself God (John 10:30-33).

prompt: Why do the sheep follow the shepherd in Jesus's comparison?
choices_json: They know his voice.
They fear the winter weather.
They can see the wolf from far away.
They are forced along by the gatekeeper.
correct_answer: They know his voice.
explanation: Jesus says the sheep follow him because they know his voice (John 10:4).

prompt: What does the contrast between the good shepherd and the hired hand highlight?
choices_json: the need for bigger shelters once winter weather reaches Jerusalem
Jesus's self-giving care versus self-protective leadership
the difference between Judea and Galilee
the value of the gatekeeper's wages
correct_answer: Jesus's self-giving care versus self-protective leadership
explanation: The good shepherd lays down his life, but the hired hand flees when danger comes (John 10:11-13).

prompt: Why does John say a division arose again among the Jews in John 10?
choices_json: The feast schedule had changed without warning.
The healed blind man returned to accuse them again.
Jesus's words about himself forced sharply different verdicts.
Roman soldiers interrupted the temple debate.
correct_answer: Jesus's words about himself forced sharply different verdicts.
explanation: John says a division arose because of these words, with some condemning him and others defending him (John 10:19-21).

prompt: How does the mention of opening the eyes of the blind affect the debate in John 10?
choices_json: It proves the earlier healing never actually happened in the first place.
It shifts the crowd to debating Moses alone.
It explains where the wolf came from.
It points back to a sign that challenges the claim that Jesus has a demon.
correct_answer: It points back to a sign that challenges the claim that Jesus has a demon.
explanation: Some answer that a demon cannot open the eyes of the blind (John 10:20-21).

prompt: According to Jesus, why do some people still not believe in John 10?
choices_json: They are not of his sheep.
They have not reached the right age yet.
They missed the Feast of Dedication.
They never met John in the wilderness.
correct_answer: They are not of his sheep.
explanation: Jesus tells them they do not believe because they are not of his sheep (John 10:26).

prompt: What function do Jesus's works serve when he speaks in Solomon's porch?
choices_json: They replace every word he has spoken.
They testify about him in his Father's name.
They remove the need for faith entirely.
They prove he is only a prophet and no more.
correct_answer: They testify about him in his Father's name.
explanation: Jesus says the works he does in his Father's name testify about him (John 10:25).

prompt: Why do many believe in Jesus beyond the Jordan at the end of John 10?
choices_json: Jesus feeds them in the wilderness there.
The temple leaders publicly endorse him at last.
John's earlier testimony matches what they now see in Jesus.
The gatekeeper from the parable explains everything.
correct_answer: John's earlier testimony matches what they now see in Jesus.
explanation: The people say John did no sign, but everything John said about this man was true, and many believed (John 10:41-42).

prompt: How do the images of the door and the shepherd work together in John 10?
choices_json: Jesus denies being a shepherd before changing his mind.
The door points to Roman authority, while the shepherd points only to the temple.
The two images cancel each other out and leave the meaning unclear.
Jesus is both the way into safety and the one who personally leads the flock.
correct_answer: Jesus is both the way into safety and the one who personally leads the flock.
explanation: Jesus says he is the door and also the good shepherd, joining access, rescue, and guidance in himself (John 10:7-11).

prompt: Within John 10, what does abundant life stand over against?
choices_json: the thief's work of stealing, killing, and destroying
the winter weather at Dedication
the long walk beyond the Jordan
the memories of John's baptism
correct_answer: the thief's work of stealing, killing, and destroying
explanation: Jesus contrasts his gift of abundant life with the thief's destructive intent (John 10:10).

prompt: What does Jesus's claim to lay down his life and take it again reveal in John 10?
choices_json: His enemies will decide both the meaning and the timing of his death.
His death will be voluntary and under the Father's command.
He can avoid death forever by leaving Jerusalem.
Resurrection belongs only to others, never to him.
correct_answer: His death will be voluntary and under the Father's command.
explanation: Jesus says no one takes his life from him; he lays it down himself and will take it again, according to the Father's commandment (John 10:17-18).

prompt: What is the significance of the 'other sheep' in John 10:16?
choices_json: Israel will be replaced by wolves from outside.
The disciples must build many rival flocks.
Jesus's flock will extend beyond the present fold into one united people.
The shepherd will divide the sheep permanently by birthplace.
correct_answer: Jesus's flock will extend beyond the present fold into one united people.
explanation: Jesus says he must bring other sheep so there will be one flock with one shepherd (John 10:16).

prompt: In the context of protecting the sheep, what does 'I and the Father are one' emphasize?
choices_json: that Jesus and the Father are simply one person speaking with one voice
that the sheep no longer need to hear Jesus's voice
that Moses and the Father now speak as one
the shared power and unity of Father and Son in guarding the flock
correct_answer: the shared power and unity of Father and Son in guarding the flock
explanation: Jesus links the Father's hand and his own hand before declaring, 'I and the Father are one' (John 10:28-30).

prompt: Why does Jesus appeal to 'I said, you are gods' when accused of blasphemy?
choices_json: He answers from their own Scripture while pressing the claim of the sanctified and sent Son.
He admits that his words in the temple were mistaken.
He denies having any special relation to the Father.
He changes the subject away from the accusation entirely.
correct_answer: He answers from their own Scripture while pressing the claim of the sanctified and sent Son.
explanation: Jesus cites their law and then asks why they accuse the one the Father sanctified and sent (John 10:34-36).

prompt: How does the ending beyond the Jordan function in John 10?
choices_json: It shows Jesus abandoning Judea forever in fear.
It confirms John the Baptist's witness as many people believe.
It reveals where the wolf from the parable lived.
It introduces a replacement feast that takes over the meaning of Dedication.
correct_answer: It confirms John the Baptist's witness as many people believe.
explanation: The chapter closes with people recalling John's true witness about Jesus and believing in him there (John 10:40-42).

prompt: How does John 10 use shepherd imagery to critique leadership?
choices_json: True leaders stay near the treasury and avoid danger.
False leaders are only foreigners from beyond the Jordan.
True leaders enter rightly, know the sheep, and give life, while false ones exploit and abandon.
Real shepherds never speak in comparisons or images.
correct_answer: True leaders enter rightly, know the sheep, and give life, while false ones exploit and abandon.
explanation: Jesus contrasts the shepherd who lays down his life with thieves and hired hands who harm or desert the flock (John 10:1, 11-13).

prompt: What grounds the assurance that no one will snatch Jesus's sheep away?
choices_json: The sheep stay safe mainly by avoiding every feast.
The gatekeeper by himself guarantees the flock's safety from every threat.
The flock can never wander or struggle at all.
Both Jesus and the Father hold the sheep with unmatched power.
correct_answer: Both Jesus and the Father hold the sheep with unmatched power.
explanation: Jesus says no one can snatch his sheep from his hand or from his Father's hand (John 10:28-29).

prompt: How does the repeated focus on the shepherd's voice shape discipleship in John 10?
choices_json: Belonging is shown by hearing Jesus and following him.
Belonging is proved by ancestry alone.
Belonging depends on winning public debates in the temple.
Belonging means never asking any question.
correct_answer: Belonging is shown by hearing Jesus and following him.
explanation: Jesus says, 'My sheep hear my voice ... and they follow me' (John 10:27).

prompt: What does 'one flock, one shepherd' contribute to the theology of John 10?
choices_json: Jesus promises many rival shepherds under one gatekeeper.
Jesus gathers a unified people from beyond the present fold.
Jesus limits salvation to one village near Jerusalem.
Jesus turns the sheepfold into a political kingdom.
correct_answer: Jesus gathers a unified people from beyond the present fold.
explanation: The saying about other sheep culminates in one flock under one shepherd (John 10:16).

prompt: How do Jesus's words and works function together in John 10?
choices_json: The works matter, but his words do not.
The words cancel any need for works.
The works confirm the claims, and the claims interpret the works.
The chapter separates what Jesus says from what Jesus does as unrelated evidence.
correct_answer: The works confirm the claims, and the claims interpret the works.
explanation: Jesus points to his works as testimony and then uses them to interpret his claims about the Father (John 10:25, 37-38).

prompt: Why does John 10 move from metaphor to attempted stoning?
choices_json: The crowd becomes confused about literal sheep prices.
The feast rules require a stoning after every parable.
Jesus asks them to stone the hired hand first.
Jesus's figurative teaching culminates in claims his opponents hear as blasphemous.
correct_answer: Jesus's figurative teaching culminates in claims his opponents hear as blasphemous.
explanation: The shepherd discourse gives way to explicit claims about Jesus and the Father, which provoke the charge of blasphemy (John 10:30-33).

prompt: How does Jesus's relationship with the Father shape the chapter's view of his death?
choices_json: He lays down his life freely in obedience to the Father's command and will take it again.
He dies only after the Father leaves him, and he has no say in what follows.
He has no authority over his death at all.
He asks John the Baptist to take his place.
correct_answer: He lays down his life freely in obedience to the Father's command and will take it again.
explanation: Jesus presents his death as both voluntary and obedient to the Father's commandment (John 10:17-18).
